tldr: Across 13 countries, headline writers uniformly led with explosion risk and mass evacuation — a near-identical safety frame varying only in scale (40,000 vs 50,000 residents) and alarm level. The sole editorial divergence visible from titles alone is the Indian press, which used its headline to surface the state-federal political clash over a Trump emergency declaration.

evidence.quote: California lucha por evitar un desastre químico
40,000 People Ordered Evacuated After SoCal Chemical Leak
Chemical blast fears trigger mass evacuation in California
Los Angeles: Chemietank droht zu explodieren

frames.sub_frame: crews racing to contain tank failure

evidence.quote: prevent explosion or leak from damaged chemical tank
crews prepare overnight operation as toxic leak keeps
fire crews race to contain toxic leak
lawmakers urge Trump to approve federal emergency declaration

single_outlet_findings.finding: Only headline in the corpus to surface the political dimension: 'California lawmakers urge Trump to approve federal emergency declaration' — frames the event as a state-federal standoff, not a chemical hazard.

Most alarming headline framing: 'Chemical blast fears trigger mass evacuation' uses 'blast fears' where peers say 'explosion risk', and appends '(VIDEOS)' — sensational register absent from all other outlets.
Only headline to foreground rising temperature as the primary story driver ('Aumenta temperatura y riesgo de explosión') rather than evacuation headcount.
Only headline to quote an official's categorical statement ('will fail') and name a federal actor (EPA chief), casting regulatory authority onto the safety narrative.
Only headline to use the word 'catastrophic' ('help avert catastrophic explosion'), elevating the severity beyond peers who say 'prevent explosion'.

bottom_line: Headline coverage is monothematic — 22 articles across 13 countries uniformly led with explosion risk and mass evacuation; the only editorial divergence visible in titles alone is India's Hindustan Times, which foregrounded California-Trump federal politics rather than the chemical hazard.
